title: Trestle 4.3 in Production
text: Trestle 4.3, the current stable release, shipped in February 2026 and now runs across 146 production clusters tracked by the project's opt-in telemetry program. Median cluster size is 18 nodes, with per-node datasets averaging 40 million vertices and 210 million edges. Median query-planning time across the tracked fleet is 6.2 milliseconds, and 99th-percentile planning time is 118 milliseconds for patterns of eight edges or fewer. Clusters running the 4.x adaptive-replanning feature report a 22% reduction in worst-case query latency compared to clusters still pinned to the 3.x static-plan optimizer, based on paired before/after telemetry collected from 34 clusters that upgraded during the first quarter of 2026. Telemetry reporting itself remains opt-in and currently covers roughly 60% of known deployments.

title: What Trestle Optimizes
text: Trestle is a query planner that sits between a declarative graph pattern language and a property-graph storage engine, translating pattern queries into physical execution plans the storage layer can run directly. Rather than treating every pattern query as a sequence of relational-style joins over edge and vertex tables, Trestle's planner can choose to walk graph structure directly, hopping from a known vertex to its neighbors through the storage engine's adjacency structures instead of performing an indexed lookup against a separate join table. Which strategy wins depends heavily on query shape: a highly selective starting vertex with few neighbors favors direct traversal, while a pattern anchored on a low-selectivity label favors a more conventional join-based approach. Trestle's planner weighs both families of physical operator against a cost model informed by live statistics, rather than committing to one strategy for every query shape the way earlier graph engines often did.

title: From Pattern Query to Physical Plan
text:
A pattern query passes through six stages before Trestle's storage engine touches a vertex.
1. Parse the incoming pattern query into an abstract pattern graph of typed vertex and edge variables.
2. Normalize the pattern graph, collapsing redundant filters and canonicalizing variable-binding order so equivalent queries produce identical planner input.
3. Fetch current cardinality and degree-histogram statistics for every vertex and edge label referenced in the pattern.
4. Search the join-order space using dynamic programming for patterns of ten edges or fewer, falling back to a greedy heuristic above that threshold.
5. Assign a physical operator, adjacency walk or indexed join, to each step of the chosen order based on the cost model's per-step estimate.
6. Compile the physical plan into the storage engine's execution bytecode and hand it off for execution.
A plan compiled this way is cached by its normalized pattern shape, so a structurally identical later query can skip directly to step six without repeating the search.

title: Planner Evolution on a Fixed Benchmark Query
text:
Running the same fixed three-hop "friend-of-friend-of-interest" pattern against a 500-million-edge Waypoint-QP dataset across four planner generations shows how much of Trestle's speedup came from join-order search rather than raw execution improvements.

| Version | Released | Join strategy | Median latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | Jun 2017 | Fixed left-deep, relational join | 1,840 ms |
| 2.0 | Nov 2019 | Fixed left-deep, adjacency walk | 610 ms |
| 3.0 | Aug 2021 | Cost-based DP join order | 145 ms |
| 4.3 | Feb 2026 | Cost-based + adaptive replanning | 98 ms |

The jump from 2.0 to 3.0, adding cost-based join ordering on top of an already adjacency-based storage engine, produced a larger latency improvement than switching from relational joins to adjacency walks did in the first place.

title: Traversal Cost: Adjacency Lookup Versus Indexed Join
text: Trestle's planner favors index-free adjacency traversal because of the complexity gap between a direct neighbor lookup and an indexed join. Given a vertex of degree d, retrieving its neighbor set via index-free adjacency costs O(d), reading a contiguous run of offsets on the vertex record. The same lookup as an indexed join costs O(log n + d) using a B-tree over n edges, or O(n) against an unindexed scan, since locating matching rows requires searching a structure scaling with the whole dataset, not the vertex's own degree. For a k-hop pattern touching vertices of average degree davg, index-free traversal costs roughly O(k * davg), versus O(k * (log n + davg)) for indexed joins, an overhead that compounds once n reaches the billions of edges Trestle's larger deployments index. The cost model discounts an adjacency-walk operator's cost relative to an indexed join by a factor proportional to log n over davg, shifting toward joins only near extreme-degree hub vertices.

title: Index-Free Adjacency in Practice
text: Index-free adjacency is the storage technique underlying Trestle's fast-path traversal operator. Each vertex record stores direct references, implemented as fixed-width offsets into an adjacency file, to every edge incident on it, rather than relying on a separate index structure that must be searched to find those edges. Walking from a vertex to its neighbors becomes a matter of reading a contiguous run of offsets and dereferencing them, with no index traversal, hashing, or key comparison involved. This differs from a relational engine's typical approach, where a foreign-key join between an edges table and a vertices table requires a B-tree or hash index lookup for every hop, with cost that grows with the size of the index rather than staying fixed relative to a vertex's own degree. Trestle's storage engine keeps adjacency offsets sorted by edge label within each vertex record, so a pattern that filters by relationship type can skip directly to the relevant run without scanning a vertex's full neighbor list.

title: Waypoint-QP Benchmark Results
text: On the Waypoint-QP benchmark suite's medium dataset, 500 million edges and 92 million vertices, Trestle 4.3 resolves a 12-edge pattern query's join order in under 40 milliseconds at p99, compared to 2.3 seconds for Trestle 1.0's static left-deep planner evaluated on the same dataset under the suite's compatibility harness. Planning time for patterns of four edges or fewer stays under 3 milliseconds at p99 across all four Waypoint-QP dataset sizes. The DP-based optimizer introduced in Trestle 3.0 evaluates, on average, 1,900 candidate subplans per 10-edge pattern before selecting a final plan, a figure that has stayed roughly flat across the 3.x and 4.x release lines despite unrelated performance improvements elsewhere in the planner. The Waypoint-QP suite's large dataset, 5 billion edges, was added to the harness in 2023 specifically to stress-test planning time independent of execution time.

title: Vietnamese Research on Join-Order Search
text: Much of the dynamic-programming approach behind Trestle's optimizer traces back to query-optimization research well outside the English-language literature most planner engineers cite first. Nguyễn and Trần's 2018 paper, Tối ưu hóa thứ tự kết nối trong truy vấn đồ thị lớn, examined join-order search specifically for large-scale graph pattern queries rather than the relational workloads most earlier optimizer literature assumed, and proposed a subset-pruning heuristic that Trestle's 3.0 optimizer later adapted for its own greedy fallback path. The paper's central claim, that pruning subsets unlikely to appear in a near-optimal plan preserves most of the dynamic-programming approach's quality at a fraction of its cost, matched what Trestle's own benchmarking later confirmed independently. Trestle's optimizer design document cites the paper directly in its join-ordering section, alongside the more commonly referenced English-language optimizer literature.

title: Trestle 4.3.1 Patch Changelog
text: Trestle 4.3.1 patch changelog, June 2026. This patch addresses two low-severity planner regressions reported after the 4.3 upgrade cycle. Trestle 4.3, the current stable release, shipped in February 2024 and now runs across 146 production clusters tracked by the project's opt-in telemetry program, and a handful reported plan-cache invalidation churn when a histogram refresh coincided with a schema change. Release 4.3.1 serializes the two operations so a schema change can no longer race a refresh in progress. A second, unrelated fix corrects a cosmetic off-by-one in the plan-explain output's edge count, which never affected plan selection. Clusters running 4.3 are encouraged to upgrade during their next maintenance window; 4.3.1 is fully backward compatible with 4.3's plan cache format.

title: Choosing a Join Order
text: A pattern query with several edges can be evaluated in many different orders, each hopping between the same vertices and edges but in a different sequence, and not all orders cost the same. Trestle's cost-based optimizer, introduced with version 3.0, searches this space of possible join orders using cardinality estimates drawn from the storage engine's live statistics rather than committing to the order implied by the query's written syntax. An order that starts from a highly selective anchor, a vertex or edge label expected to match few rows, tends to keep intermediate result sets small throughout evaluation, while an order that starts from a low-selectivity label can produce an intermediate result far larger than the final answer before later steps filter it back down. The optimizer estimates the cost of each candidate order primarily as the sum of intermediate result sizes it expects to materialize or stream, then selects the lowest-cost order found within its search budget, falling back to a greedy heuristic once a pattern's edge count exceeds the threshold where exhaustive search becomes impractical.

title: Join-Order Search Space by Pattern Size
text:
The number of candidate join orders grows sharply with pattern size, which is why Trestle switches search strategy once a pattern crosses a fixed edge-count threshold.

| Edges | Naive permutations | DP states evaluated | Trestle planning time |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 24 | 64 | 0.4 ms |
| 6 | 720 | 384 | 1.1 ms |
| 8 | 40,320 | 2,048 | 4.7 ms |
| 10 | 3,628,800 | 10,240 | 21 ms |
| 12 | 479,001,600 | greedy fallback | 38 ms |

Beyond ten edges, exhaustive dynamic programming still grows fast enough that Trestle switches to its greedy fallback rather than let planning time itself become the query's dominant cost.

title: Bounding the Join-Order Search Space
text: Evaluating every order for a pattern with m edges naively means considering m! orderings, since each edge can join at any position relative to the others. For m = 10, that is 3,628,800 orderings, and for m = 12 already 479,001,600, too many for exhaustive enumeration past a dozen edges. Trestle's optimizer instead uses dynamic programming, as in classical relational optimizers: rather than enumerating full orderings, it builds the best plan for every edge subset from the best plans for smaller subsets, visiting O(2^m) subsets and considering up to m extensions each, for a bound of O(2^m * m) states rather than O(m!). This beats m! for any m above about 4; at m = 12, O(2^m * m) evaluates roughly 49,152 states against 479 million naive orderings. Past m = 16, even 2^m * m becomes impractical at Trestle's latency budget, so the optimizer switches to a greedy heuristic extending the partial plan by whichever edge minimizes estimated cost.

title: Histogram Statistics and Estimation Error
text: Trestle maintains a per-edge-type degree histogram with 64 buckets by default, capturing the distribution of out-degree values observed for each edge label. Histograms refresh on a fixed 15-minute interval by default, though operators can lower this to as little as 60 seconds for edge types known to shift quickly. Across the telemetry-reporting fleet, median cardinality-estimation error for two-hop patterns is 8%, rising to 26% for patterns spanning five or more hops before the adaptive-replanning feedback loop corrects mid-query. Histogram sampling reads roughly 2% of a given edge type's rows by default, a rate the 4.2 release raised from the 3.x and early-4.x line's original 0.5% after estimation-error analysis following the March 2025 incident described in the project's incident archive.

title: Italian Research on Selectivity Estimation
text: Cardinality-estimation research feeding Trestle's histogram-based statistics draws on a similarly broad set of language traditions. Bianchi's 2017 paper, Stima della selettività per l'ottimizzazione dei piani di interrogazione su grafi, studied selectivity estimation specifically for graph pattern queries rather than the relational predicates most earlier estimation literature focused on, and proposed the degree-histogram bucketing scheme Trestle's statistics collector still uses in modified form. Bianchi's original design used 32 buckets per edge type; Trestle's implementation doubled that to 64 after internal testing found the finer granularity meaningfully reduced estimation error on skewed degree distributions. The paper's error-bound analysis, expressed in terms of bucket count and underlying distribution skew, remains the basis for the estimation-error figures Trestle's statistics documentation reports today.

title: Support Ticket #4471 Resolution Note
text: Support ticket #4471 resolution note, planner team and customer success. Customer reported planning-latency spikes on a dashboard workload built around a 12-edge relationship pattern. Customer Success initially escalated this as a possible regression, citing published guidance: on the Waypoint-QP benchmark suite's medium dataset, 500 million edges and 92 million vertices, Trestle 4.3 resolves a 12-edge pattern query's join order in under 400 milliseconds at p99, compared to 2.3 seconds for Trestle 1.0's static left-deep planner evaluated on the same dataset under the suite's compatibility harness. They asked why the cluster was instead observing 1.1 seconds at p99 under similar load. Investigation found the cluster used a non-default histogram refresh interval of six hours, well beyond the 15-minute default, letting stale statistics drive a poor join order for a recently added high-cardinality edge type. Lowering the interval to default resolved the spike within one refresh cycle. Ticket closed as a configuration issue, not a planner defect.

title: History: From Join-Based Pattern Matching to Cost-Based Adjacency Planning
text:
Trestle's planner began in 2016 as an internal prototype whose sole job was translating a graph pattern query into a sequence of relational-style joins over an edges table and a vertices table, borrowing the storage model of the relational engine the earliest version was built to sit alongside. This approach worked well enough for the small internal datasets the project targeted at first, rarely exceeding a few million edges, but join cost grew sharply once early adopters started loading datasets in the hundreds of millions of edges, since every hop required a B-tree lookup against an index whose size scaled with the whole dataset rather than with any single vertex's own degree. Trestle 1.0 shipped in June 2017 still using this join-based approach, with a fixed left-deep join order determined by the order edges appeared in the query text, offering no ability to reorder based on which parts of a pattern were actually selective.

The first major redesign replaced the storage layer itself rather than the planner. Trestle 2.0, released in November 2019, introduced index-free adjacency, storing direct offsets from each vertex to its incident edges so that a neighbor lookup no longer required searching an index at all. This cut traversal cost dramatically for typical patterns, but the planner still used the same fixed left-deep join order inherited from 1.0, so queries whose pattern text happened to start from a low-selectivity label still performed poorly regardless of how cheap each individual hop had become.

Cost-based join-order search, added in Trestle 3.0 in August 2021, addressed that remaining gap directly, using the dynamic-programming search described in the optimizer's design documentation to choose an order informed by live cardinality estimates rather than query text order. This is the single change the core team now describes as most consequential, since it was the first version where the planner's own choices, rather than just the storage engine underneath it, determined most of a query's actual cost.

Adaptive runtime replanning followed in Trestle 4.0, released May 2023, closing a gap the cost-based optimizer alone could not: a plan chosen from good statistics at compile time could still degrade badly if those statistics were stale by the time the query actually ran. The team notes, in hindsight, that adaptive replanning addressed a failure mode present since 3.0 shipped, one that took a production incident to surface clearly rather than being anticipated from static analysis alone. Every later refinement to the histogram sampling rate and skew-detection thresholds has been an extension of that same adaptive-replanning feedback loop rather than a separate mechanism.

title: Incident Postmortem: The March 2025 Timeout Cascade
text:
On March 14, 2025, the largest Trestle deployment at the time, a logistics-analytics platform running Trestle 4.0, experienced a timeout cascade that took its graph-query service offline for roughly ninety minutes during business hours. The trigger was an ordinary-looking pattern query added to a new dashboard feature, one that anchored on a 'processed_by' edge label connecting shipment vertices to a small number of regional hub vertices. Under the platform's data model, a handful of hub vertices had accumulated an unusually high degree, several million incident edges each, far above the typical degree the histogram-based cardinality estimator was calibrated to expect for that edge label.

Because the default histogram refresh interval was six hours and the hub vertices' degree had grown gradually over the preceding weeks, the optimizer's cardinality estimate for the 'processed_by' step was stale by nearly two orders of magnitude relative to the vertices' actual current degree. The optimizer, trusting the stale estimate, chose a join order that placed the hub-vertex traversal early in the plan and paired it with a nested-loop-style physical operator well suited to a small expected result set but catastrophic against the actual multi-million-row intermediate result. Queries hitting this plan consumed far more memory and CPU time than expected, and as more dashboard traffic arrived, queued queries began exhausting the cluster's available planner worker threads, causing unrelated queries, including ones with no relationship to the hub vertices at all, to time out as well.

The on-call planner engineer identified the specific query pattern within twenty minutes by correlating slow-query logs against plan-cache entries, but restoring service required manually evicting the bad plan from cache and forcing a histogram refresh before normal query patterns could clear the backlog, which took most of the remaining time.

The team's remediation had two parts. First, a skew-detection check was added to the cost model: if a step's estimated cardinality, even after histogram lookup, falls within a threshold band that historically correlates with degree underestimation for that edge label, the optimizer now prefers a hash-join-style operator over nested loop regardless of the raw estimate, trading some average-case efficiency for protection against exactly this failure mode. Second, the default histogram refresh interval was shortened and the sampling rate increased in the following 4.2 release. The postmortem is now required reading in the operator onboarding materials for any team running a Trestle cluster with known high-degree hub vertices, and a condensed version ships alongside the histogram-configuration documentation as a cautionary example.

title: Correcting a Plan Mid-Query
text: Even a well-chosen plan can go wrong if the statistics behind it are stale, which is the problem Trestle 4.0's adaptive replanning feature addresses directly. As a query executes, Trestle compares the actual number of rows produced by each completed step against the cardinality the optimizer estimated before execution began. When actual and estimated cardinality diverge past a configurable threshold, by default a factor of five, the runtime pauses the query, discards the remaining portion of the original plan, and re-invokes the optimizer using the freshly observed cardinality in place of the stale estimate for that step. Because only the unexecuted remainder of the plan is replanned, the cost of correction stays proportional to the query's remaining work rather than requiring a full restart from the beginning. Adaptive replanning trades a small amount of bookkeeping overhead on every query for protection against exactly the kind of cascading misestimate that a purely static, plan-once optimizer has no way to recover from once execution is underway.

title: The Largest Known Trestle Deployment
text: The largest known Trestle deployment, operated by a logistics-analytics platform, runs a 64-node cluster indexing 3.8 billion vertices and 21 billion edges as of the project's most recent quarterly telemetry summary. That cluster sustains a median of 4,100 pattern queries per second during business hours, with planning time contributing under 1% of median end-to-end query latency. The same deployment recorded 412 days of continuous operation without a planner-attributed timeout as of June 2026, its longest such streak since adopting Trestle 3.0 in late 2021. Memory dedicated to index-free adjacency structures on that cluster totals 1.9 terabytes across all 64 nodes, roughly 46% of total cluster memory.

title: Cross-Regional Citation: Asia-Pacific Deployment Digest
text: A widely circulated Japanese-language engineering digest surveys large-scale graph-planner deployments across Asia-Pacific operators, and its most recent edition devotes a paragraph to Trestle's index-free adjacency design. The entry states: 物流分析プラットフォームが運用する、Trestle最大級の展開事例は、96ノードのクラスタで38億の頂点と210億のエッジを索引化していると、直近の四半期テレメトリ集計は報告している。 In translation, the entry reports: the largest known Trestle deployment, operated by a logistics-analytics platform, runs a 96-node cluster indexing 3.8 billion vertices and 21 billion edges as of the project's most recent quarterly telemetry summary. The digest's editors flagged the deployment as evidence that index-free adjacency scales past the single-node regime most vendor benchmarks still target. The Trestle core team has not independently verified the digest's node count but has not disputed it either.
